mappings.rationaleAI: Rhetoric from the Sophists through Cicero and Quintilian covers the Sophists and Socrates (A.1.b), the Roman ideal of humanitas and training of the orator (A.1.f).
Medieval universities with their trivium and quadrivium curriculum directly address the codification of the liberal arts (A.2.b) and the founding of the universities (A.2.c).
Humanism from Cicero's humanitas through Renaissance and modern forms covers the Roman ideal (A.1.f), the idea of Renaissance (A.3.a), and humanism and the new learning (A.3.b).
The 12th-century Renaissance with recovery of classical texts, founding of universities, and scholastic philosophy covers the founding of universities (A.2.c) and the Scholastic method (A.2.d).
Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelianism and Christian theology addresses the Scholastic method (A.2.d) and faith and reason (A.2.e).
The history of education from Greek paideia covers Homeric education and the ideal of the hero (A.1.a), the codification of liberal arts (A.2.b), and the rise of the liberal arts college (A.3.g).
Virtue from Socrates through Aristotle and Aquinas covers the Sophists and Socrates (A.1.b), Plato and the Academy (A.1.c), and Aristotle's ethics (A.1.d).
Raphael's School of Athens depicts ancient philosophers in a High Renaissance context, embodying the idea of Renaissance (A.3.a) and humanism and the new learning (A.3.b).
Al-Kindi's role in the translation movement bringing Greek philosophy into Arabic connects to the Islamic tradition of humanistic scholarship (A.5).
The Abbasid Caliphate's House of Wisdom and translation movement preserving Greek texts address the Islamic tradition of humanistic scholarship (A.5).
The concept of heroism from Homer's Iliad and Odyssey addresses Homeric education and the ideal of the hero (A.1.a).
The concept of the individual from Renaissance humanism through Enlightenment covers the dignity of the free individual (A.3.a) and diverse views of the humanities (B.1).
Science and religion tensions address the Christianization of pagan culture (A.2.a) and the separation of science from philosophy (A.3.d).
The philosophical concept of authenticity from Rousseau through existentialism connects to the concept of the dignity of the free individual (A.3.a) and theories of the humanities as knowledge (B.3).
The emergence of the professional scientist traces the growth and proliferation of special disciplines (A.3.h) and the profession of learning.
Comenius's educational philosophy and vision of pansophia connect to humanism and the new learning (A.3.b) and the rise of educational institutions (A.3.g).
Plato's Republic covering the Theory of Forms, education, and the relationship between knowledge and authority addresses Plato and the Academy (A.1.c).
